Title: Mother's 11-year fight to secure stateless daughter's identification documents continues
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: For 59-year-old Tinara Sahri, the struggle to secure a legal identity for her daughter has stretched for years, with repeated applications and rejections leaving the family in limbo.
Tinara, who is a permanent resident originally from Indonesia, said she has been trying to resolve the status of her 17-year-old adopted daughter, Lailatul Kamalia Abdul Rahim, since birth.
The
New Straits Times
met the pair at a press conference by the Malaysia International Humanitarian Organisation (MHO) today on the issue of statelessness and the proposal of extending the MyKAS temporary resident identity card to solve the crisis.
Born in Malaysia, Lailatul, does not possess a MyKad, despite having official documents including a birth certificate and adoption registration document.
Tinara said she had made numerous trips to the National Registration Department and the Welfare Department in an effort to regularise her daughter's status.
"I've been going up and down… NRD, Welfare (Department). Then they told me to change the adoption certificate. After that was approved, they asked me to apply for citizenship," she recounted.
Her first citizenship application was submitted in 2015, but it has since faced multiple rejections.
"Rejected, failed, rejected, failed. I applied again and again. Even now, I'm still applying," she said.
According to Tinara, the reasons given were often vague.
"They say there are not enough requirements (met)," she said.
The prolonged uncertainty has also affected her daughter's education.
Lailatul had to stop schooling after Form Three as she does not have an identity card, and is now unable to continue and sit for the Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia examination this year.
The situation has taken an emotional toll on Tinara.
"I'm already tired… really tired," she said.
With both her own parents no longer alive, Tinara said she is facing the situation largely on her own.
She said she is not particular about the type of identification granted to her daughter, as long as she is legally recognised.
"Red IC (permanent resident) or blue IC (Malaysian citizen), I'm grateful. Even red is fine. As long as she has an identity card," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MHO backs MyKAS card for stateless people to address documentation crisis
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A humanitarian organisation has called on the Home Ministry to extend the MyKAS temporary resident identity card to stateless people as a practical measure to address a long-running documentation crisis that poses risks to both the affected individuals and national security.
Malaysian International Humanitarian Organisation (MHO) secretary-general Datuk Hishamuddin Hashim said the proposal is not intended to confer citizenship automatically, but rather to ensure that every stateless individual has a recorded identity that can be tracked by the authorities.
"What MHO is asking, appealing to the government, is that the stateless community today, who meet criteria that may be determined, be given the relaxation necessary to obtain a document called MyKAS," he said at a press conference at MHO's office here today.
He said the organisation is urging the government to improve and relax the eligibility conditions under the existing mechanism used by the National Registration Department (NRD), specifically for applications for MyKAS.
He said MHO was encouraged to raise the issue after Kuamut state assemblyman Datuk
Masiung Banah
publicly supported issuing the MyKAS card to stateless or undocumented people in Sabah.
"Recently, a statement was issued by the Sabah mufti stating that on the basis of Islam and humanity, the stateless community must be documented.
"When politicians usually speak, we can say there are political interests. But this time the statement was made by the mufti, speaking on humanitarian grounds about the need for the stateless community to have some form of document," he added.
Also present were MHO's Sabah coordinator Jerry Jaimeh and legal adviser May Eva Sofea Zainal Abidin.
Jerry said on the
statelessness
crisis in Sabah in particular, the vast majority of individuals involved comprise Suluk and Bajau refugees who fled conflict in southern Philippines in the 1970s, as well as those whose communities migrated from North Kalimantan between modern-day Malaysia and Indonesia before either country existed.
These refugees, he said, were granted the IMM13 pass, among other documentation schemes, as a way to formally recognise their presence.
Currently, he added, to apply for MyKAS, applicants must not have any parents who held the IMM13 pass, a condition that effectively bars the children of IMM13 holders from applying, given that issuance of the IMM13 pass was frozen in March 2013, leaving many children in statelessness limbo.
Today, based on the organisation's fieldwork, stateless people in Sabah are estimated at about 1.2 million.
Hishamuddin highlighted that any relaxation of MyKAS eligibility must be accompanied by stringent control mechanisms, including comprehensive security screening, biometric data collection and periodic monitoring by enforcement agencies.
The organisation said the absence of identity documents among stateless people is already creating security vulnerabilities, making it difficult for authorities to track individuals involved in criminal activity and creating openings for cross-border crime.
"We have no other choice. Whether we like it or not, we simply cannot remove millions of stateless individuals from Malaysia.
"Where would we put them? Send them to the Philippines? They do not have Philippine citizenship. Send them to Indonesia? Indonesia has never recognised them as Indonesian citizens.
"We cannot dump them in the sea. Like it or not, this is a reality we must accept. I know this topic is not a popular one to discuss," Hishamuddin said.
The organisation said resolving the crisis is not new, pointing to
proposals
made around February and June 2022 by then-deputy chief minister of Sabah Datuk Seri Jeffrey Kitingan and then-home minister Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainuddin to introduce a foreign national card for stateless people.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's general insurance gross written premiums rise to RM24.2bil in 2025
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's general insurance industry maintained its growth trajectory last year, albeit a slower pace, after expanding 4.8 per cent compared with nearly seven per cent previously.
The General Insurance Association of Malaysia (PIAM) said the industry had posted gross written premiums of RM24.2 billion in 2025, up from RM23.1 billion a year earlier.
PIAM chief executive officer Chua Kim Soon said the expansion was primarily supported by its two largest segments, motor and fire insurance.
"Together, these two segments make up about 66 per cent of the overall portfolio," he said at a media briefing today.
PIAM said underwriting profit increased to RM1.2 billion, up RM125 million year-on-year, with the overall combined ratio holding steady at about 93 per cent.
Motor insurance remained the largest segment of the general insurance market, accounting for 45.2 per cent of total premiums, followed by fire insurance at 20.9 per cent.
Despite its dominant share, the motor segment continued to record underwriting losses amounting to RM289.3 million, with its combined ratio standing at 103 per cent.
PIAM noted that non-motor segments particularly fire, marine, aviation and transit, as well as personal accidents contributed positively to overall underwriting performance.
Fire insurance, in particular, posted a strong underwriting profit of RM700.8 million, with a combined ratio of 69.5 per cent.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Guidelines boost jobs, tackle underemployment among neurodivergent individuals
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Guidelines for Employment Support for Neurodivergent Individuals are expected to drive skill-based career pathways while addressing underemployment among persons with learning disabilities.
Deputy Human Resources Minister Datuk Khairul Firdaus Akbar Khan said the guidelines, an initiative by the Social Security Organisation (Perkeso), serve as a strategic framework to strengthen inclusive workforce participation.
He said underemployment within the disabled community remains a major challenge for the Human Resources Ministry, requiring immediate and coordinated intervention from multiple stakeholders.
Citing the Global Disability Inclusion Report 2025, Khairul Firdaus said the issue could result in losses to national productivity, particularly affecting Gross Domestic Product (GDP) in low- and middle-income countries.
"Persons with disabilities, including those who are neurodivergent, are actively seeking employment. Data from the MYFutureJobs portal shows that 5,482 neurodivergent jobseekers have registered to date.
"However, we must acknowledge that the main challenge does not lie in individuals' abilities, but rather in the readiness of our labour market to provide appropriate support, including workplace adjustments, flexibility and more structured support systems.
"The role of the government today goes beyond merely providing job opportunities; we are actively building an employment ecosystem that is inclusive and responsive," he said.
He was speaking at the launch of the Guidelines for Employment Support for Neurodivergent Individuals and the exchange of a memorandum of understanding (MoU) between Perkeso and the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) here today.
The guidelines were developed to serve as a reference for employers, to enhance understanding of neurodiversity as well as employment support for neurodivergent individuals. They provide examples of practical support based on research findings and input on neurodiversity in the workplace obtained from various stakeholders.
Khairul Firdaus said the MoU reflects the madani government's commitment to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly in education, employment, economic growth and reducing inequality, especially for neurodivergent individuals.
"We are establishing a 'core triangle' of employability, which forms a key foundation for empowering the sustainable participation of neurodivergent individuals in the national labour market.
"These guidelines are the first of their kind in Malaysia and will serve as a national benchmark for developing more inclusive employment policies and programmes for persons with disabilities, particularly those who are neurodivergent," he said.
He said neurodivergent individuals in Malaysia should not be viewed merely as persons with disabilities, but rather recognised as national assets with unique potential, particularly in high-impact sectors.
In Malaysia, a total of 333,474 individuals are classified as having learning disabilities, with a significant proportion being neurodivergent.
"As such, this strategic framework to enhance workforce participation among neurodivergent individuals focuses on evidence-based guidelines, targeted service interventions, data-driven job matching, employer engagement and strategic collaboration with international bodies.
"This is the approach we are taking—not just providing access to employment, but building a comprehensive support system that enables these individuals to grow in their careers," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Palm oil rally seen continuing on biodiesel demand boost, analyst Mistry says
Article:
MUMBAI: Malaysian palm oil prices are likely to rise about 12 per cent to 5,200 ringgit (US$1,316) a metric ton by mid-July, as higher energy prices from the US.-Israeli war on Iran boost biodiesel demand and tighten supplies, analyst Dorab Mistry said on Wednesday.
The benchmark palm oil contract on the Bursa Malaysia Derivatives Exchange fell 1.34 per cent to 4,647 ringgit at the midday break on Wednesday, though it is up about 15 per cent since the war began in late February.
Palm oil futures are expected to extend gains to around 5,000 ringgit by June and potentially reach 5,200 ringgit by mid-July on biodiesel demand, said Mistry, the director of Indian consumer goods company Godrej International.
One of the most closely watched analysts of edible oils, Mistry's forecasts for supply and prices often move markets.
Global oil prices hit a four-year high of more than US$126 a barrel last week. This rally has made the use of vegetable oils for biofuel production more attractive.
Refined fuels like diesel and gasoline rose more sharply than crude after the Iran war began, Mistry said.
As a result, the spread between fossil diesel and palm biodiesel narrowed, cutting subsidy requirements and - in some markets - making palm biodiesel cheaper than fossil diesel, he said.
"Rising energy prices prompted Indonesia to reinstate its B50 palm biodiesel programme from 1 July 2026," Mistry said. "Biodiesel mandates are being increased in other countries like Malaysia, Thailand and others too."
Indonesia, the world's biggest palm oil producer, said it would raise the mandatory blending rate for palm-based biodiesel to 50 per cent from 40 per cent on July 1.
Palm oil competes with soyoil, which has rallied in recent weeks as top producers - the US, Brazil and Argentina - increase its use for biofuels.
"The US has announced its long-awaited jumbo biodiesel programme for 2026 and 2027, which has, as expected, lit a fuse under soybean oil futures," Mistry said.
Higher edible oil prices are leading to demand destruction in key consuming countries like India, where stocks have fallen and imports will need to be stepped up from June, he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Elderly driver in fatal crash released on police bail
Article:
PETALING JAYA: An elderly man involved in a crash with a motorcycle that resulted in the rider's death yesterday has been released on police bail on health grounds.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner Shamsudin Mamat said the man in his 70s was detained to assist in investigations before being released on police bail.
"Checks found that the elderly man has a valid driving licence. Initial urine and alcohol screening tests conducted on him were also negative.
"He was detained and released on police bail on health grounds due to his age and medical condition," he said when contacted today.
He said the incident is believed to have been caused by the negligence of the elderly man, who was driving a Proton X70 and lost control of the vehicle.
"At the time of the incident, the driver was on his way home, while the victim, who was a food delivery rider, was working," he said.
Yesterday, it was reported that a motorcyclist died after being involved in a crash with a car at Jalan 17/21 here.
Shamsudin said initial investigations found that the accident occurred at 1.45pm involving a Proton X70 driven by the elderly man and a motorcycle ridden by a man in his 20s.
He said preliminary findings showed the driver lost control of the vehicle before veering into the opposite lane and colliding with the motorcycle, causing severe injuries to the rider, who was pronounced dead at the scene.
The driver was unhurt and was taken to the Petaling Jaya district police headquarters for further action.
The case is being investigated under
Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act
for causing death by reckless or dangerous driving.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: South Korean judge who hiked ex-first lady's jail sentence found dead
Article:
SEOUL: A South Korean judge who more than doubled the former first lady's prison sentence last month was found dead on Wednesday, police said.
Shin Jong-o was "found unconscious around 1 am (1600 GMT on Tuesday)... at the Seoul High Court building", an investigator at Seocho district police station told AFP.
Shin was taken to hospital and pronounced dead, he said, adding: "There is no sign of foul play in the death."
Local media reported that Shin had left a suicide note, but the investigator said there was none.
Last month, Shin presided over 53-year-old Kim Keon Hee's appeal trial, finding her guilty of stock manipulation and bribery, and increasing her sentence to four years from 20 months.
The heavier sentence came after her acquittal by a lower court on the stock manipulation charge was overturned.
Shin said at the time that Kim had "failed to acknowledge her culpability and has instead consistently resorted to excuses."
The police investigator said on Wednesday that the judge's "bereaved family is stricken by the incident" and requests privacy.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police arrest four suspects over fatal armed robbery in Teluk Intan
Article:
TELUK INTAN: Police have arrested four suspects in connection with a gang armed robbery that resulted in the murder of an elderly man in Pekan Baru here on April 17.
Hilir Perak district police chief Assistant Commissioner Chua Kok Lian said the suspects, aged between 20 and 30, were arrested on April 23 around Teluk Intan and Bentong, Pahang.
He said RM8,000 worth of jewellery robbed from the victim's house was also recovered despite being pawned, in addition to the seizure of other case items, including several vehicles and two parangs.
"Acting on intelligence, police arrested seven suspects to assist in the investigation at the initial stage, but only four were directly involved in the murder.
"The other three suspects are involved in other robbery and cable theft cases that also occurred around Teluk Intan," he said at a press conference at the Hilir Perak district police headquarters here today.
Chua said all seven local suspects have past criminal records involving violence, property crimes, and drug abuse, with urine screening tests finding five of them positive for methamphetamine.
He added that the four suspects are being investigated under Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder, as well as Section 457 of the same code for housebreaking.
He said the other three suspects, believed to be involved in a housebreaking case and six cable theft cases in Teluk Intan, causing losses estimated at RM200,000, are being investigated under Sections 379 and 457 of the Penal Code.
"The investigation paper for the fatal robbery has been referred to the Attorney-General's Chambers, and the four suspects are expected to be charged under Section 302 of the Penal Code at the Teluk Intan Court tomorrow.
"Police are now tracking down another male suspect wanted for the case, identified as Sri Shaileswara Sri Vengkateswara Babu," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US wants 'concrete actions' on Iran from next Iraqi PM
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States is looking for "concrete actions" by Iraq's next prime minister to distance the state from pro-Iran armed groups before resuming financial shipments and security aid, a senior official said Tuesday.
Iraq's ruling coalition has put forward Ali al-Zaidi as the next leader and he quickly received a congratulatory call from President Donald Trump, who had threatened to end all US support if former frontrunner Nouri al-Maliki took office.
But a senior US State Department official, speaking on condition of anonymity, said Zaidi must address the "blurry line" between pro-Iran armed groups in the Shia-majority country and the state.
Washington suspended cash payments for oil revenue, which have been handled from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York in an arrangement dating to the aftermath of the 2003 US invasion of Iraq, as well as security assistance over a spate of attacks on US interests.
Resuming full support "would start with expelling terrorist militias from any state institution, cutting off their support from the Iraqi budget (and) denying salary payments to these militia fighters," the official said.
"Those are the type of concrete actions that would give us confidence and say that there's a new mindset."
The official said US facilities in Iraq suffered more than 600 attacks after February 28, when the United States and Israel launched their war on Iran.
The attacks have come to a standstill since a shaky April 8 ceasefire between the United States and Iran, with the exception of Iranian strikes in Iraqi Kurdistan.
"I'm not underestimating the severity of the challenge or what it would take to disentangle these relationships. It could start with a clear and unambiguous statement of policy that the terrorist militias are not part of the Iraqi state," the official said.
"Certain elements of the Iraqi state have continued to provide political, financial and operational cover for these very terrorist militias," he added.
The United States piled pressure on Iraq after it appeared that Maliki would be the next prime minister.
During his previous stint in office, relations deteriorated with Washington over accusations of being too close to Iran's Shia clerical government and fanning sectarian flames.
Attacks by armed groups in Iraq have struck the US embassy in Baghdad, its diplomatic and logistics facility at the capital's airport and oil fields operated by foreign companies.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Welder gets 33 years jail, 32 strokes of rotan for raping stepdaughter
Article:
PASIR MAS: A welder was sentenced to 33 years' jail and 32 strokes of the rotan by the Sessions Court here today after pleading guilty to seven counts of raping and injuring his stepdaughter between 2022 and April this year.
Judge Zulkpli Abdullah handed down the sentence after the 40-year-old man pleaded guilty to all charges.
For the first and second charges, the man was charged with raping his stepdaughter, who was 14 years and four months old at the time, in June 2022 and April 2023.
The offences took place between 12am and 1am at a house in Pengkalan Kubor, Tumpat.
He was charged under Section 376(3) of the Penal Code, which provides for a prison sentence of up to 30 years and at least 10 strokes of the cane.
For the third to sixth charges, the man was charged with committing incest against the same victim at the same location between 11pm and 3am between May 2025 and April 23 this year.
These charges were framed under Section 376B(1) of the Penal Code, which carries a prison term of up to 30 years and whipping.
For the seventh charge, the man was charged with voluntarily causing hurt to the victim at the same location about 11pm on April 29.
This charge was under Section 323 of the Penal Code, read together with Section 326A. Section 323 provides for a jail term of up to one year or a fine of up to RM2,000, while Section 326A allows for a prison term of twice the maximum period.
Deputy public prosecutor Siti Aisyha Nailah Harizan prosecuted while the man was unrepresented.
Earlier, Siti Aisyha Nailah pressed for a deterrent sentence, highlighting that the victim was the man's stepdaughter and had been abused since she was 14.
The man pleaded for leniency.
The court ordered the 33-year jail term and 32 lashes for all charges to run from the date of his arrest on April 24.
The court also ordered the man to undergo counselling throughout his imprisonment and placed him under police supervision for two years upon completion of his sentence.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Education Ministry finalising new school safety guidelines
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Education Ministry is finalising new school safety guidelines aimed at strengthening protection measures in schools nationwide.
The updated guidelines are being developed with input from the Education Institution Safety Reform Committee following several recent accidents that have raised concerns over student safety.
In a statement today, the ministry said it is also consulting with experts, including the National Institute of Occupational Safety and Health Malaysia, particularly on improving protocols involving the use of vehicles within school compounds.
"In the interim, all schools under the ministry have been instructed to strictly comply with the existing Safe School Management Guidelines currently in force.
"School administrators have also been reminded to consistently emphasise safe vehicle use within school premises to prevent further incidents," the statement read,
The ministry also stressed that schools must remain a safe environment for students, teachers and the wider school community.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Padini probe: Eight held as MACC investigates frozen bank accounts
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has detained eight people to assist in investigations linked to the freezing of several bank accounts belonging to clothing retailer Padini Holdings Bhd and its subsidiaries.
MACC chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the investigation is ongoing, with statements already recorded from 10 people.
He said another 15 people would be brought in to assist in the probe.
"The case is still under investigation," he told reporters at a press conference today.
Azam said the anti-graft agency will continue freezing the company's assets despite applications by related parties seeking their release.
"However, the investigating officers are of the view that the company's assets still need to remain frozen," he said.
It was reported that MACC had frozen certain bank accounts of Padini Holdings Bhd and its subsidiaries in connection with an ongoing investigation.
The garment retailer said in a Bursa Malaysia filing that the accounts were frozen under Section 44(1) of the Anti-Money Laundering, Anti-Terrorism Financing and Proceeds of Unlawful Activities Act 2001.
The company said it is assisting in the ongoing investigation and that the affected accounts are not actively used for the group's day-to-day operations.
On April 28, Padini Holdings Bhd said 21 bank accounts belonging to the company and five of its subsidiaries had been frozen by the MACC involving external counterparties.
The company, in a filing with Bursa Malaysia, said
most of the frozen bank accounts are under Padini Corporation Sdn Bhd
, which has seven accounts, followed by Yee Fong Hung (Malaysia) Sdn Bhd and Padini Dot Com Sdn Bhd with four accounts each.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Father remanded for alleged sexual assault against 12-year-old daughter
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A palm oil labourer has been remanded for seven days starting today for suspected physical sexual assault against his daughter since last year.
Magistrate Nurasidah A Rahman issued the remand order against the 53-year-old suspect until Tuesday, enabling police to investigate the case under Section 14(a) of the Sexual Offences Against Children Act 2017.
Based on the report, the 12-year-old student's ordeal was uncovered after she underwent a health check-up at her school.
The medical team noticed the student was depressed and withdrawn, and upon asking about her situation, the girl revealed that she had been a victim of sexual assault by her biological father since last year.
The victim, who was living with her aunt following her parents' divorce, said the most recent incident took place on May 3 at the suspect's house in the district.
She added that she had also been shown pornographic videos since February.
The victim was subsequently referred to the Sultanah Nora Ismail Hospital (HSNI) for an examination before a police report was lodged yesterday.
Following the report, officers from the district police headquarters' Special Investigation/Serious Crime Division arrested the suspect at 10.35pm last night and seized his mobile phone to assist in the investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rules on fully-imported EVs tightened, ministry raises entry bar for imports
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry has introduced new requirements for fully imported (CBU) electric vehicles, effectively raising the threshold for market entry on both price and performance.
From July 1, CBU EVs imported into Malaysia must carry a minimum cost, insurance and freight (CIF) value of RM200,000 and deliver at least 180 kW (around 241 hp).
The revised criteria were communicated in a circular to franchise approved permit (AP) holders, according to paultan.org.
The new framework replaces the existing benchmark of a RM250,000 minimum selling price and 200 kW output requirement.
While the lower headline price threshold may appear more lenient, CIF value refers to the vehicle's landed cost before taxes, duties and dealer margins.
This means the updated structure is expected to squeeze out a range of mid-tier EV models, leaving the CBU segment more skewed towards higher-end offerings.
Assuming that a fully imported car has the minimum CIF value of RM200,000, it will attract a minimum of five per cent import duty, 10 per cent excise duty and 10 per cent sales tax bringing the effective cost for the distributor up to RM250,000.
Factor in a rough estimated margin of 10 per cent for the distributor/principal and 10 per cent for the dealer, the recommended retail price could hit at least RM300,000.
The portal said the circular also stated that, in addition to the new CIF and power output requirements, CBU EVs imported into Malaysia must still comply with the earlier set of conditions issued on Dec 29 last year, which includes a minimum floor price of RM100,000.
While largely a theoretical risk, this layered framework could potentially allow room for regulatory arbitrage if importers choose to declare higher CIF values.
Any such inflation would also result in higher taxes and duties being payable to the government, as these are calculated based on the declared CIF.
Should any CBU EV models enter the market well below the RM300,000 mark after July, it would likely indicate that manufacturers have absorbed the additional cost burden to remain competitive under the new rules.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pahang, Singapore deepen ties, explore investment and tourism cooperation
Article:
KUANTAN: Pahang Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Wan Rosdy Wan Ismail described the courtesy visit by the Singaporean delegation led by Senior Minister Lee Hsien Loong as a meaningful platform for discussions to further strengthen bilateral ties.
He said the meeting today provided a valuable platform to strengthen close relations between Pahang and Singapore, while paving the way to explore new areas of cooperation for mutual benefit.
Wan Rosdy highlighted that cooperation in areas such as investment, economic development, tourism, education and strategic collaboration would benefit both parties.
"Strong ties that have already been established between Pahang and Singapore will continue to be enhanced for shared prosperity, while creating more opportunities for cooperation that will positively impact the people and the state's development," he said in a Facebook post today.
Wan Rosdy was accompanied by State Secretary Datuk Seri Nazri Abu Bakar, State Financial Officer Datuk Fadzilla Salleh, State Legal Adviser Datuk Muhammad Saifuddin Hashim Musaimi, Pahang Public Works, Transport and Health Committee chairman Datuk Ir Razali Kassim, and Pahang Investment, Industry, Science, Technology and Innovation Committee chairman Datuk Mohamad Nizar Najib.
The Singaporean delegation included Minister of State in the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and the Ministry of Social and Family Development Zhulkarnain Abdul Rahim, member of parliament Joan Pereira, Singapore's High Commissioner to Malaysia Vanu Gopala Menon, and officials from Singapore's Ministry of Foreign Affairs.
Yesterday, Lee, who is on a five-day visit to Pahang and Terengganu until May 8, had
an audience with the Sultan of Pahang
, Al-Sultan Abdullah Ri'ayatuddin Al-Mustafa Billah Shah, at Istana Abdulaziz here.
On May 5, Lee and a 41-member delegation visited the Malayan Tiger Conservation Centre in Lanchang, Temerloh, before touring the iconic murals at Kuantan Art Street and visiting Kuantan 188, Malaysia's second-tallest freestanding tower after the Kuala Lumpur Tower.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Four months on the run, mechanic charged with murder of elderly man
Article:
NIBONG TEBAL: After four months on the run, a mechanic was charged in the magistrate's court in Jawi here today with the murder of an elderly man last December.
C. Jagadesh, 34, nodded in understanding after the charge was read to him before magistrate Nurul Ainna Ahmad.
No plea was recorded as the case falls under the jurisdiction of the High Court.
Jagadesh was charged with murdering Goh Aik Lai, 60, at BK Foodvillage, Taman Jelatik, Sungai Bakap, about 4pm on Dec 30, 2025.
The charge was framed under Section 302 of the Penal Code, which provides for the death penalty or imprisonment of between 30 and 40 years, along with not fewer than 12 strokes of the cane, upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Airina Syazreen Zainurin prosecuted while the accused was represented by lawyer Puveneswaran.
The court set July 17 for mention pending post-mortem, chemistry and DNA reports.
Bail was denied and the accused was remanded in custody.
It is understood the accused fled to a neighbouring country after the incident before he was arrested on April 30.
On Dec 30, it was reported that one man was killed while two others were injured after being attacked by a group of men armed with parangs at
a restaurant
in Sungai Bakap.
Debt issues are believed to have been the motive behind the attack.
Following the incident, police arrested 14 men in several raids in the state and Perak to assist in investigations.
Penang police chief Datuk Azizee Ismail had said that preliminary investigations found those detained were believed to be members of a secret society active in the state.
Azizee said checks also showed that 10 of the suspects had prior criminal and drug-related records.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PBAPP to install two main pipelines to boost water supply in Penang
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Penang Water Supply Corporation (PBAPP) will install two 1.8m diameter main pipelines to channel more treated water from the Sungai Dua Water Treatment Plant (WTP) to the Barat Daya district on the island as part of efforts to strengthen the water supply by 2030.
PBAPP chief executive officer Datuk K. Pathmanathan said the project is a key component of Phase 3 of the Water Contingency Plan 2030 (WCP 2030), with an estimated total cost of about RM416 million involving pipe procurement, installation works, excavation, road reinstatement works, and land acquisition costs.
He said the first pipeline will connect the Sungai Dua WTP to the connection point of the Penang Third Undersea Twin Pipeline Project (3rd PTSP) in Butterworth, involving a route of about 13km on the mainland.
"The second 1.8m diameter pipeline will connect the 3rd PTSP landing point in the Macallum area to the Bukit Dumbar Reservoir and Pump House Complex over a distance of about 3.9km on the island for RM56 million," he told reporters after inspecting the project site at Solok Tengku here today.
Pathmanathan said works for the new pipeline from Macallum to Bukit Dumbar are scheduled to begin this month and are expected to be completed by May next year due to technical and logistical constraints involving traffic management and the relocation of utilities, such as Tenaga Nasional Bhd (TNB) and telecommunications lines.
"In addition, with the cooperation of the Malaysian Highway Authority, the Public Works Department and local authorities, we are confident these challenges can be overcome and the project completed as soon as possible," he said.
He said the 3.9km project will be implemented in two phases due to land acquisition constraints, with the second phase involving about one kilometre crossing Sungai Pinang.
According to him, about 400 million litres of water per day are currently channelled to the island, and with the completion of the dedicated pipelines, the capacity can be increased to around 600 million litres per day.
He added that the overall RM2.1 billion WCP 2030 is being implemented to optimise raw water resources, increase water treatment capacity, and reduce dependency on the Sungai Dua WTP.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: TH: Queue system and quota allocation remain sole criteria for haj eligibility
Article:
MAKKAH: The queue system and quota allocation are the main criteria used by Lembaga Tabung Haji (TH) to determine Malaysian pilgrims' eligibility, independent of financial capacity or higher package payments.
Licensed haj operators (PJH) operations director Anwar Ahmad said all prospective pilgrims, including those applying through PJH packages, are subject to the same processes and criteria set by TH.
He noted that there is a misconception among some members of the public that paying for more expensive packages can expedite their queue position or improve the chances of a successful haj appeal.
"That is not true, as all appeals are processed through TH's system based on priority and the current quota, not on the price of the package selected. Package payments only cover additional services after a person has been approved to perform the haj," he told reporters here yesterday.
Anwar said the approach is crucial to ensure that the principles of fairness and equality are upheld in the management of Malaysian pilgrims, given the high annual demand compared with the limited quota allocated.
He said this situation has also been exploited by irresponsible parties spreading misleading information, including claims of guaranteed or expedited approval through certain packages.
"Syndicates also use various tactics such as promoting packages on social media, offering exclusive deals, and claiming to have special access to attract prospective pilgrims.
"Therefore, the public is reminded that haj approvals are based on a transparent system and cannot be manipulated by any party," he said.
Anwar added that prospective pilgrims should verify all information through TH's official channels, including its website, call centre, or by visiting any TH branch office before making any decisions or payments.
"Ensure that every haj offer comes from TH and only deal with licensed haj operators to avoid the risk of fraud," he said.
Meanwhile, he said TH will continue to strengthen the monitoring of PJH companies to ensure all services offered comply with requirements and safeguard the welfare of pilgrims throughout their stay in the Holy Land.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lim Guan Eng trial: No money trail did not weaken probe, court told
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The absence of a money trail in Lim Guan Eng's corruption trial involving the Penang undersea tunnel project did not undermine the integrity of the investigation, the Sessions Court heard today.
The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) senior investigating officer Zulhilmi Ramli acknowledged that there was no money trail in the case as the alleged payments to the former Penang chief minister were made in cash.
"In my investigation, I found that the payments were made in stages and in cash and not deposited or transferred into any account.
"However, this did not undermine my investigation as it was supported by witness testimonies and documentary evidence," he said during examination-in-chief by lead prosecutor Datuk Wan Shaharuddin Wan Ladin.
He said this when testifying as a prosecution witness in Lim's corruption trial involving the construction of a major road and undersea tunnel project in Penang worth RM6.3 billion.
It was revealed during the trial that the DAP advisor had received millions in cash from Consortium Zenith Construction Sdn Bhd (CZCSB) director Datuk Zarul Ahmad Mohd Zulkifli's car on Aug 20, 2017.
CZCSB was the company awarded the RM6.34 billion Penang undersea tunnel project.
The money was allegedly handed to the Bagan member of parliament in stages through middleman Datuk G. Gnanaraja.
Zulhimi said Zarul's statement was corroborated by several other witnesses including Gnanaraja, CZBUCG former senior vice-president and director Azli Adam and Ibrahim Sahari, respectively.
He said all of them confirmed cash withdrawals and meetings between Zarul and the accused.
"The statements are consistent and support each other," he said.
Zulhilmi also defended the MACC's decision to call Gnanaraja as a witness, despite suggestions that his statements contained contradictions.
He said Gnanaraja's account was aligned with Zarul's statement recorded in late 2019, in which Zarul admitted delivering four bags containing RM1 million each.
Zulhilmi said Gnanaraja, in a statement recorded around mid-2020, also admitted receiving four bags containing RM1 million each from Zarul, totalling RM4 million.
"This formed part of the basis of the MACC's investigation," he said.
Zulhilmi said his investigation also uncovered documentary and forensic evidence, including proof of meetings allegedly held inside a car involving Zarul, Gnanaraja and Lim.
"This is supported by forensic reports, including photographs taken inside the car and at a house," he added.
Lim is facing four charges of using his position as the then Penang chief minister to solicit gratification to help a company secure the undersea tunnel project.
He is alleged to have sought 10 per cent of the profit to be made by the company.
He is also accused of receiving RM3.3 million for himself and causing two plots of state government land to be disposed of to two companies linked to the undersea tunnel project.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: India's cows offer biogas alternative to Mideast energy crunch
Article:
ACROSS much of India, an energy crunch caused by the Iran war has prompted long queues for cooking gas cylinders. That's not a problem for Gauri Devi.
On a stove with blue flames, she flips a chapati flatbread, burning biogas produced from cow dung – an alternative fuel helping ease pressure on supplies.
"It cooks everything," the 25-year-old said in her courtyard kitchen in Nekpur, a village in Uttar Pradesh, about 90 kilometres (55 miles) from New Delhi. "If the pressure goes down, we let it rest for half an hour and it works again."
India consumes more than 30 million tonnes of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) annually, importing over half its needs.
The government insists there is no shortage of cooking gas, but supply delays, panic buying and black marketeers have created long queues for cylinders.
However, since the 1980s India has also promoted biogas as a low-cost rural energy source, subsidising more than five million "digester" units that convert farm waste into methane for cooking, and nitrogen-rich slurry for fertiliser.
For Gauri, it requires mixing a couple of buckets of dung with water, then pouring the mixture into a car-sized underground tank topped with a storage balloon.
It provides a piped methane supply so regular that she only uses an LPG cylinder for emergencies or large gatherings.
The biogas works for everything – "vegetables, tea, lentils", she said.
The residual slurry is later spread on fields as fertiliser. It has better nitrogen availability for plants compared with raw dung, farmers say.
"The manure is so good," said farmer Pramod Singh, who installed a larger unit in 2025, enough for six people, fuelled by 30-45 kilogrammes of dung daily from four cows.
And he said the slurry fertiliser is particularly valuable at a time when global supplies of artificial fertilisers have been hit by trade disruptions due to the war.
"The real benefit is not just the gas – that is like a bonus," local farmer leader Pritam Singh said. "The slurry is 'black gold'."
More than 45 per cent of India's 1.4 billion people rely on farming, and the country has one of the largest cattle populations.
[[nid:1433476]]
India – the world's most populous nation and third-largest fossil fuel polluter – has pushed large-scale biogas production to achieve a goal of carbon neutrality by 2070.
The government last year required that biogas account for at least one percent of liquid gas fuelling both vehicles and for domestic use – rising to five percent by 2028.
Dozens of multi-million dollar production plants are now in the pipeline.
But small-scale rural producers are also being rolled out – units cost around 25,000-30,000 rupees (US$265-US$318), often heavily subsidised by the government.
In a Hindu-majority nation where cows are revered and dung and urine are used in everything from floor plastering and fuel to ritual practices, it is easy to win supporters, said Pritam Singh.
He installed his first plant in 2007, and has helped put in 15 more in his village in the past year alone.
He said interest had shot up after the LPG shortages.
"People who earlier were not interested now ask how to get it," he said.
"Once they see food being cooked and crops benefiting, they are convinced."
But biogas is still a small fraction of household cooking fuel – with LPG considered more convenient because companies manage the supply chain.
"Biogas plants are not just equipment; they are mini factories," said A.R. Shukla, president of the Indian Biogas Association.
"They need organised installation, regular operation and maintenance," he added.
"So, unless installation and upkeep are handled through community-based or cooperative enterprises, households will continue to treat biogas as secondary fuel."
And even with government support, there are barriers to uptake, including cost and space.
"We work on other people's farms the whole day. We don't have land for it," said labourer Ramesh Kumar Singh, standing in a line of around 100 queueing for LPG cylinders in the nearby village of Madalpur.
"I am standing in scorching heat, hungry and thirsty," said Mahendri, 77, who had failed to secure a cylinder for three days in a row.
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tengku Permaisuri of Selangor urges public to learn from global tensions
Article:
SHAH ALAM: The Tengku Permaisuri of Selangor, Tengku Permaisuri Norashikin, has urged the public to draw lessons from ongoing global geopolitical tensions and their impact on economic and social stability.
Expressing concern over the situation, she called on the public to stay alert to current developments, understand national priorities, and be prepared to face uncertainties with wisdom and responsibility.
"I call on all Malaysians to continue praying that our country and the region are protected from the adverse effects of these conflicts," she said at the 104th UiTM Convocation Ceremony at the Dewan Agung Tuanku Canselor today.
Earlier, Her Royal Highness conferred postgraduate degrees on 290 graduates at the doctoral and clinical specialist master's levels. Also present was UiTM vice-chancellor Prof Datuk Dr Shahrin Sahib@Sahibuddin.
Amid rapid technological changes and shifts in the global economic landscape, she advised graduates to remain adaptable, think critically, and stay competitive while upholding values such as integrity and responsibility.
She also expressed appreciation to UiTM for its role in developing the nation's talent, stressing that the institution must uphold its mandate in safeguarding the rights of the Malays, Orang Asli, and natives of Sabah and Sarawak in line with constitutional provisions.
"It is our shared responsibility to ensure UiTM continues to play its role in upholding the special position of the Malays and natives of Sabah and Sarawak, while safeguarding the legitimate interests of other communities as provided for in the Constitution.
"UiTM must remain true to this mandate, and I do not consent to any attempt to alter or undermine its founding purpose," said Tengku Permaisuri Norashikin, who is also the UiTM pro-chancellor.
She congratulated former Sabah state secretary Datuk Kee Mustafa on his appointment as UiTM pro-chancellor, expressing hope that he will continue to contribute to the university's excellence.
She also congratulated the 25,336 graduates on completing their studies and thanked their parents and families for their support.
She expressed hope that the graduates would uphold the university's reputation as its ambassadors, noting that their achievements mark the beginning of greater challenges ahead.
She added that UiTM must continue to produce knowledgeable, ethical, and patriotic graduates who are capable of contributing to the nation's future.
"As a multicultural nation, we must continue to foster mutual respect across religions and cultures to preserve unity and well-being," she said.
The 104th UiTM Convocation Ceremony runs from today until May 23.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MACC pursuing US$2b more in 1MDB asset recovery efforts
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) is optimistic of recovering another US$2 billion (about RM7.9 billion) in assets linked to the 1Malaysia Development Bhd (1MDB) scandal through ongoing international asset recovery efforts.
MACC chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said authorities are currently pursuing several major recoveries involving funds and assets held by individuals and entities connected to the scandal.
He said among the recoveries being pursued are approximately US$800 million linked to a person, and another estimated GBP100 million tied to a separate party.
"There are more funds we are trying to recover. For example, one individual involves about US$800 million, while another may involve around GBP100 million.
"There are also several other ongoing recovery processes. Hopefully, another US$2 billion can still be recovered," he told a press conference at MACC headquarters today.
Azam, however, did not disclose the identities of the individuals involved due to ongoing legal and investigative processes.
He said MACC will continue working closely with foreign enforcement agencies and governments to trace and recover assets connected to the misappropriation of 1MDB funds.
The latest projected recovery comes after Malaysia successfully recovered RM31.3 billion, or 74.5 per cent, of the RM42 billion believed to have been misappropriated from 1MDB.
Azam described the achievement as among the highest asset recovery rates globally for a large-scale financial crime scandal.
He said international standards for financial crime asset recovery generally range between 60 and 70 per cent.
"International standards are not necessarily about recovering 100 per cent. Generally, if authorities can recover between 60 and 70 per cent, that is already considered significant.
"So if we can exceed that figure, it is something we can be proud of," he said.
Azam said Malaysia's recovery efforts have involved coordinated cooperation with international partners including the United States Department of Justice (USDOJ), the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), and authorities in several other jurisdictions.
He said the recovery exercise includes settlements involving international financial institutions such as Goldman Sachs, Aabar Investments PJS (AIPIC), and Deloitte and KPMG-linked entities, which collectively contributed billions of ringgit back to Malaysia.
Apart from cash recoveries, MACC has also been pursuing luxury assets and artworks purchased using funds linked to the scandal.
Azam said 12 artworks have been identified for recovery, with four already successfully brought back to Malaysia while another eight are expected to be returned by the end of this year.
He reiterated that MACC remains committed to tracing and recovering all remaining assets linked to 1MDB regardless of jurisdiction or how long the process may take.
"This effort is not only about recovering the country's funds, but also restoring public confidence," he said.
In January, it was reported that Malaysia was expected to recover  1MDB funds from several people, including PetroSaudi chief executive Tarek Obaid and the family of fugitive businessman Low Taek Jho, also known as Jho Low, who were previously found to have received misappropriated money.
Among the funds being pursued by the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) is RM600 million from Tarek.
Accounts belonging to Jho Low's family are also expected to be returned to the government in the near term. The MACC currently anticipates that approximately
RM5 billion
will be repatriated.
1MDB was a sovereign fund set up in 2009 with the help of Malaysian financier Jho Low to promote economic development.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Azam Baki: More govt officials may face MACC scrutiny
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has indicated that more government officials could come under scrutiny following recent investigations involving two director-generals of government agencies.
MACC chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the commission continuously monitored and verified information involving public officers before opening formal investigations.
"I can say this, whether public officers or otherwise, there are names that come to our attention, but we will verify first," he said during a press conference at the MACC headquarters here today.
Azam stressed that MACC would not investigate individuals based solely on allegations circulating on social media or unverified claims.
"We are not an agency that immediately investigates people based on slander or speculation on social media.
"If there are reports or verified information, MACC will investigate if the matter falls under our jurisdiction," he said.
His remarks came following recent MACC actions against two director-generals linked to separate abuse of power and misappropriation investigations.
Azam also reiterated MACC's long-standing warning to civil servants against corruption and abuse of power.
He said public officers entrusted with positions of authority must not be involved in corruption or leakages that harmed the country.
"Civil servants, especially those holding positions of trust, cannot be involved in corruption and abuse of power that result in leakages in our country," he said.
Azam said corruption and abuse of power were often linked to other forms of criminal activity.
"Corruption and abuse of power are so much related to other criminal activity," he said.
He also highlighted the scale of financial leakages suffered by the country, saying Malaysia had lost RM277 billion over a six-year period.
Azam said about RM5 billion had been recovered over the past two-and-a-half years through enforcement and asset recovery efforts.
He added that MACC and other enforcement agencies under the Multi-Agency Task Force (MATF) framework would continue working together to recover stolen assets and combat corruption.
"Asset recovery is the latest approach that needs to be actively implemented across all enforcement agencies, including Customs, police, Bank Negara and the Inland Revenue Board," he said.
Azam said foreign authorities had continued cooperating with Malaysia in 1MDB-related recovery efforts because of MACC's commitment to pursuing the case since 2015.
"It is not easy, but bringing back RM33 billion so far is a major achievement in Malaysia's history," he said.
It was previously reported that the
director-general of the Survey and Mapping Department
faced two charges at the Sessions Court here on April 22 over the alleged misuse of power to obtain funds for personal gain.
On April 3, Department of Environment director-general
Datuk Wan Abdul Latiff Wan Jaffar pleaded not guilty
at the Sessions Court to accepting a RM100,000 bribe linked to an environmental approval process in Penang.
The 57-year-old was accused of corruptly receiving the cash from a company director through an intermediary as an inducement to consider an appeal for exemption from preparing an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) report for the construction of a dual carriageway at Bukit Kukus, Paya Terubong.
The alleged offence was committed at his office on Jan 15 last year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Floods force 49 Raub villagers to evacuate homes
Article:
RAUB: Villagers in Kampung Batu Talam here were caught off guard when continuous heavy rain in the early hours of today caused floods that inundated their homes.
Water began entering the houses about 7am after Sungai Kapur Batu Talam in the settlement burst its banks, forcing families to evacuate to the SMA Al-Falah hall.
A Raub Civil Defence Force (APM) spokesman said 49 people from 16 families were relocated in stages, with the process still ongoing.
"Heavy rain since 2am caused the river to overflow into the low-lying settlement, leaving homes submerged under up to 0.5 metres of water.
"The victims were instructed to evacuate as a precaution in case the situation worsened," he said in a statement.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] MACC proposes charging two over RM1.1bil semiconductor probe
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has proposed charging two persons over the RM1.1 billion investment deal involving the Economy Ministry and UK-based firm ARM Limited.
MACC chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the investigation papers were nearing completion and are expected to be submitted to the deputy public prosecutor soon.
He said investigators had recorded statements from 22 witnesses, including political analyst James Chai and former Economy Minister Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli.
"Investigations are almost at the final stage. At this point, there are only two more witnesses we need to record statements from, one in Taiwan and another locally," he told reporters at the MACC headquarters today.
Azam said the probe was being conducted under Section 23 of the MACC Act 2009 for abuse of power, Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating, and Section 409 of the Penal Code for criminal breach of trust.
"We expect the investigation papers to be submitted to the deputy public prosecutor in the near future. It will then be up to the Attorney-General's Chambers to decide whether there is a case to prosecute," he said.
Azam disclosed that MACC investigators had identified two individuals whom they would recommend for prosecution.
"At this stage, we have identified two individuals whom we will propose to be charged in court.
"But I am not saying they will definitely be charged. Investigators can recommend charges if the evidence fulfils the elements under the law, but the final decision rests with the Attorney-General," he said.
However, Azam declined to reveal the identities of the two individuals.
"I am not going to mention any names here. Whoever wants to speculate, that is up to them. We do not speculate on individuals. That is our ethics," he said.
When asked whether the two individuals were James Chai and Rafizi, Azam again refused to comment.
Rafizi is currently
being questioned
by MACC investigators for the third consecutive day at the commission's headquarters in Putrajaya.
Azam said MACC officers were focused solely on establishing whether criminal elements existed in the case.
"My officers are looking into criminality. If there is evidence, it is up to the Attorney-General to decide on further action," he said.
He added that the investigation process would proceed according to established procedures before any charging decision is made.
It was reported that the
investigation was focused on alleged
irregularities linked to the RM1.1 billion investment arrangement during Rafizi's time as economy minister.
On Monday, the Pandan member of parliament was questioned for more than eight hours.
He told reporters that the session proceeded smoothly and that he had given his full cooperation to investigators.
However, Rafizi declined to comment further on the scope or direction of the investigation.
Last week, Rafizi's former aide, James Chai, was questioned for three consecutive days to assist in the same investigation.
The MACC opened the investigation paper on Feb 16 following complaints lodged by several non-governmental organisations.
The NGOs alleged that the agreement was rushed and one-sided, resulting in potential financial implications for the government.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysian Pacific Industries poised for robust three-year growth
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysian Pacific Industries Bhd (MPI) is expected to deliver a robust three-year core net profit at a compound annual growth rate of 28 per cent supported by stronger contributions from the industrial segment.
CIMB Securities noted that MPI's earnings will also be lifted from the acquisition of Carsem Semiconductor Bangkok Ltd.
The company's US dollar revenue grew 31.6 per cent to US$465 million in the first nine months, driven by higher contributions across Asia, the US and Europe.
However in ringgit terms, revenue growth was slower at 22.1 per cent year-on-year (YoY), primarily owing to negative forex impacts, which resulted in a RM14.4 million forex loss during the period.
Despite the headwinds, MPI's net profit rose 30.4 per cent YoY to RM144 million, supported by strong demand for power module packages driven by AI infrastructure rollouts, gradual recovery in the automotive segment and robust utilisation at Carsem Ipoh and Carsem Suzhou.
"Overall, we consider the results to be in line with expectations as we anticipate a stronger fourth quarter driven by higher utilisation of power packages for next-generation artificial intelligence server applications, alongside continued recovery in automotive demand," said the firm.
CIMB Securities maintained its "Buy" call on the stock with a target price of RM41.
In a separate note, RHB Research said MPI's Malaysia and China operations are expected to reach optimal utilisation by the second half of 2026, supported by consumer, server and sensor-related packages.
"Additional upside stems from a rebound in automotive demand, silicon carbide or SiC, robotics power packages, and a NOR flash pivot in Thailand," it added.
The firm kept its "Buy" call on MPI with a higher target price RM43.80 from RM35 previously.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Man arrested after drug possession, police chase ends in three-car crash
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A 31-year-old man found in possession of drugs was arrested after fleeing police and causing a three-car crash at a traffic light along Jalan Ampang near Gleneagles Hospital yesterday.
Kuala Lumpur Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Zamzuri Mohd Isa said the suspect, who was driving a white Perodua Bezza, failed to stop when instructed by an Ampang police patrol unit, triggering a pursuit from Pandan Indah to the scene of the incident.
"Upon reaching Jalan Ampang, the suspect is believed to have lost control of his vehicle and crashed into two other vehicles, a Perodua Aruz driven by a 42-year-old Malay woman and another Perodua Bezza driven by a 35-year-old Malay man, both of whom were stationary at a red traffic light.
"The accident resulted in damage, with no injuries reported," he said in a statement, adding that the man was later apprehended at the scene by policemen.
Earlier footage of the incident went viral, showing the damaged white Bezza after crashing into other vehicles, with a police patrol unit seen at the scene with its sirens blaring.
"Further checks found 4.7g of Yaba pills in his possession. A urine test also showed that he tested positive for amphetamine and methamphetamine," he said.
He said the suspect has been taken to the Ampang district police headquarters for further action.
"The case is being investigated under Section 186 of the Penal Code for obstructing a public servant in the discharge of their duties, which carries a maximum jail term of two years, a fine of up to RM10,000, or both, upon conviction.
"The Kuala Lumpur Traffic Police are also investigating the case under Section 42(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for driving a vehicle on a road recklessly or in a manner dangerous to the public.
"Wangsa Maju police have also opened investigations under Section 12(2) for possession of dangerous drugs and Section 15(1)(a) for self-administration of dangerous drugs under the Dangerous Drugs Act," he said.
He said a remand application has been made at the Ampang magistrate's court to facilitate further investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] 1MDB recovery hits RM31.3bil, among world's highest in financial scandals
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has recovered RM31.3 billion, or 74.5 per cent, of the RM42 billion misappropriated from 1Malaysia Development Bhd (1MDB).
MACC Chief Commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the marks one of the highest asset recovery rates globally for a large-scale financial crime scandal.
He said the amount exceeded earlier projections of a 70 per cent recovery rate, which would have been about RM30 billion.
"Based on available data, the total amount misappropriated from 1MDB was RM42 billion.
"As of 2025, assets and funds worth about RM31.3 billion have been recovered.
"The 1MDB asset recovery rate is considered a major achievement and has received international recognition because, according to international standards, recovering between 60 and 70 per cent is already regarded as extraordinary," he told reporters at the MACC headquarters today.
Azam said Malaysia had recovered assets worth tens of billions of ringgit, including proceeds from the sale of the luxury yacht Equanimity valued at about RM543 million, as well as the return of private jets and overseas properties.
He said Malaysia had also reached global settlements with international financial institutions such as Goldman Sachs, International Petroleum Investment Company (IPIC), and Aabar Investments PJS, as well as audit firms Deloitte and KPMG, which collectively contributed billions of ringgit.
"The return of funds by other individuals and entities, including through cooperation with the United States Department of Justice (USDOJ), has further strengthened this achievement.
"These efforts were carried out through mechanisms such as mutual legal assistance, financial intelligence sharing, bilateral discussions, and cross-border asset forfeiture actions involving countries including the United States, Switzerland, the United Kingdom, and Singapore," he said.
Azam said the commission remains committed to tracing and recovering every asset misappropriated, regardless of its location or how long the process takes.
"This effort is not only aimed at restoring the country's financial resources, but also at restoring the dignity of the nation and public confidence in state institutions.
"No one will escape legal action and every sen belonging to the people will be ensured to be returned," he said.
In January, it was reported that Malaysia expected to recover 1MDB funds from several individuals, including PetroSaudi chief executive Tarek Obaid and the family of fugitive businessman Low Taek Jho, commonly known as Jho Low.
The commission is pursuing about RM600 million from Tarek.
Accounts belonging to Jho Low's family are also expected to be returned to the government in the near term, with the MACC anticipating that about RM5 billion will be repatriated.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Google scientist says EU data measures pose privacy risk for users
Article:
BRUSSELS: A top Google scientist sent a warning to EU antitrust regulators on Tuesday that its proposal requiring the company to share search engine data with rivals such as OpenAI risked exposing users' private information, the sternest rebuke yet in a tussle over Google's lucrative business model.
The European Commission, which acts as the EU competition enforcer, has in recent years cracked down on Big Tech via a slew of legislation to ensure that users have more choices and smaller rivals room to compete that has however triggered the ire of the US government.
Sergei Vassilvitskii, with the title of distinguished scientist at Google since 2012 and regarded a leader in his field, will meet EU antitrust officials on Wednesday to voice his concerns and propose a broader approach with better guardrails.
The meeting comes a month after the Commission outlined a series of steps that Google should take to allow rival search engines access search data such as ranking, query, click and view data on fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory terms.
The EU proposal, which will be finalised in the coming weeks following feedback from interested parties, has triggered a furious response from Google which called it regulatory overreach that could jeopardise users' privacy and security.
The issue is the Commission's proposed method to ensure anonymised personal data, Vassilvitskii said, underlining fears that this may not be strong enough to prevent modern AI tools from sifting through the data to identify people.
"We are concerned because the EC's approach to anonymisation fails to protect Europeans' privacy: our red team managed to re-identify users in less than two hours," he said in exclusive written comments to Reuters.
Google's AI red team is a group of hackers which simulate a variety of realistic adversary activities to highlight potential vulnerabilities and weaknesses and come up with fixes.
"We are eager to share our technical expertise and work with the EC to establish the right guardrails and protect Europeans from privacy harm," Vassilvitskii said.
Regulators will decide by July 27 on the exact measures which Google will have to implement.
Failure to do so could see the company charged with breaching the Digital Markets Act which seeks to rein in the power of Big Tech and penalised with a fine that could be as much as 10 per cent of its global annual revenue.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RT Pastry to issue 91.5mil new shares en route to Bursa listing
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bakery specialist RT Pastry Holdings Bhd has signed an underwriting agreement with KAF Investment Bank Bhd for its initial public offering (IPO) on the Ace Market of Bursa Malaysia.
RT Pastry specialises in the manufacturing and retail of premium pastry and bakery products, operating over 17 outlets in the Klang Valley.
The IPO entails a public issue of 91.5 million new shares, representing about 27 per cent of the company's enlarged share capital.
RT Pastry said 16.9 million of the new shares will be allocated for the public, 6.7 million shares for eligible persons of the company and 42.3 million shares for the private placement to Bumiputera investors approved by the Investment, Trade and Industry Ministry.
The remaining 25.4 million shares will be reserved via private placement to institutional and selected shareholders.
RT Pastry executive director and group chief executive officer Lu Chun Neng said the IPO will provide it with the platform to further expand its retail network and upgrade its manufacturing capabilities.
RT Pastry plans to use the listing proceeds for the opening of new outlets, acquisition of new machinery and equipment as well as repayment of bank borrowings.
KAF Investment is the principal adviser, sponsor, underwriter and place agent for the exercise.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Van driver remanded three days over death of Year Two pupil in Batu Pahat
Article:
BATU PAHAT, May 6 — A 60-year-old van driver has been remanded for three days starting today to assist police in the probe into the death of a Year Two pupil from a primary school in Parit Sulong here.
Batu Pahat police chief Assistant Commissioner Shahrulanuar Mushaddat Abdullah Sani said the remand application for the man was made to assist investigations under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act (APJ) 1987.
“The remand order on the suspect was issued by Magistrate Nurasidah A. Rahman from today to May 8, after he was brought to the Batu Pahat Magistrate’s Court earlier this morning,” he said when contacted by the media today.
Earlier, the van driver was arrested after being involved in a fatal accident that claimed the life of an eight-year-old girl in front of SK Seri Maimon in Parit Sulong here.
It was reported yesterday that Nur Aini Umairah Mohd Fareez was killed after she was hit by a delivery van at 12.30pm while walking in front of the school’s main gate.
As a result of the accident, the victim suffered serious head and bodily injuries.
She was later pronounced dead at the scene by medical personnel from the Parit Sulong Health Clinic.

Title: Azam Baki: MACC recommends charges against two in Arm Holdings chip probe
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, May 6 — The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has recommended two people involved in the chip patent deal with UK semiconductor giant Arm Holdings to be charged, its outgoing chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said today.
“Two people. I can’t tell you who, but we are recommending that they be charged,” he said at a press conference to unveil four of the dozen artworks bought with siphoned money from 1MDB at MACC headquarters here.
“But whether or not they will be charged, it’s not up to us. If they (the Attorney General Chambers) don’t think they should be, then okay, but on our side, we recommended the two be charged,” Azam added.
MORE TO COME

Title: MACC targets recovery of RM8b in remaining 1MDB assets, nearly 75pc already reclaimed
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 6 — The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) is still aiming to recover close to RM8 billion in stolen 1MDB assets, its outgoing chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said today, as the agency claimed success in repatriating up to 75 per cent of the siphoned assets and monies as of 2025.
A large chunk of the assets still unrecovered are tied to certain individuals, including one said to hold up to RM2 billion in stolen funds, Azam told a press conference here.
“In all, I think there’s about US$2 billion (RM7.9 billion) still to be recovered,” Azam told a press conference here.
“Some of them are tied to individuals and other entities. Some US$800 million is believed to be held by one person and another about £100 million (RM536 million),” he added.
The agency said it has recovered at least RM31.3 billion, or 74.5 per cent, of the RM42 billion misappropriated from investment fund 1MDB as of 2025, hailing it as among the highest asset recovery rates globally for a large-scale financial crime case.
The asset recovery efforts began in 2019, with RM1.155 billion retrieved that year, followed by RM14.257 billion in 2020 and RM5.117 billion in 2021.
“Subsequently, RM400.38 million was recovered in 2022, RM8.088 billion in 2023, RM715.95 million in 2024 and RM1.566 billion in 2025,” Azam said.

Title: MACC detains eight in Padini-linked probe, statements recorded from 10
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, May 6 — The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has detained eight individuals to assist in investigations linked to the freezing of several bank accounts belonging to garment retailer Padini Holdings Bhd, and its subsidiaries.
MACC chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki said the probe is ongoing, with statements recorded from 10 individuals so far, while another 15 are being traced to assist investigations.
“The case is still under investigation,” he told a press conference here today.
He said that the asset freeze remains in place despite requests from related parties for the accounts to be released.
“Investigating officers are of the view that the company’s assets should continue to be frozen,” he said.
On April 25, the company said it has initiated an internal review following the freezing of several bank accounts belonging to the company and its subsidiaries by the MACC, which it said was linked to investigations involving certain external counterparties to the group who are not its employees, officers or management.
The company also said it had engaged external legal counsel and taken necessary steps, including applying for relief to unfreeze the accounts. — Bernama

Title: Johor to form school safety task force after Year Two pupil killed by van in Batu Pahat
Article:
ISKANDAR PUTERI, May 6 — The Johor state government will set up a special task force to look into safety in schools, following a Year Two pupil’s death after she was hit by a delivery van in Batu Pahat yesterday.
Johor education and information committee chairman Aznan Tamin said the initiative was aimed at investigating and improving students’ safety inside and outside school premises.
He said this also included school-based activities.
“The task force will also propose solutions and improve the existing guidelines already in place,” he told reporters when met at the Johor state legislative assembly in Bangunan Sultan Ismail in Kota Iskandar here today.
It was reported yesterday that eight-year-old Nur Aini Umairah Mohd Fareez was killed after she was hit by a delivery van in front of the fenced area of SK Seri Maimon in Parit Sulong, Batu Pahat.
Aznan, who is the Tanjung Surat assemblyman, said the task force is expected to start a safe school campaign early next month, which will continue throughout the year.
He said the existing standard operating procedure involving vehicles entering and leaving school premises would be reviewed in detail.
On Nur Aini Umairah’s death, Aznan said the Johor Education Department is expected to provide counselling assistance to the victim’s family.
“The counselling assistance is to help ease the psychological impact suffered by the victim’s family,” he said, adding that other forms of assistance will also be channelled to ease their burden.

Title: Deeper Malaysia–Philippines partnership crucial to shaping Asean’s future, says former Yang di-Pertua Negeri of Penang
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, May 6 — Malaysia can play a vital role in shaping Asean’s future by working closely with the Philippines, the current Asean chair, to advance key regional initiatives, said former Yang di-Pertua Negeri of Penang Tun Ahmad Fuzi Abdul Razak.
He said that among the proposed regional initiatives are the Digital Asean Commons, aimed at integrating digital economies, data governance and innovation ecosystems across the region, as well as the Regional Supply Chain Resilience Initiative, which aims to position Asean as the world’s most reliable and diversified manufacturing hub.
“Other initiatives include Asean Green Transition Partnership, which aims to mobilise public and private sector investment in sustainable energy and climate resilience, and the Asean Talent Mobility Framework to facilitate the movement of skilled professionals across borders,” he said in his keynote address at the Asean Leadership and Partnership Forum (ALPF) 2026 here today.
He also highlighted Track 1.5 and Track 2 diplomacy platforms to bring together governments, businesses and academia to anticipate and respond to geopolitical shifts for the benefit of Asean.
“All these initiatives require not only government support and leadership, but also private sector dynamism and regional collaboration, with entrepreneurship as the driving force,” he added.
Touching on Asean centrality, Ahmad Fuzi said it must be understood in practical terms, noting that it is not only about convening but also about strategic coordination, credibility and coherence.
At the national level, Malaysia needs to strengthen economic resilience and diversification, invest in digital transformation and green growth and build human capital for future industries, while at the regional level, he said Malaysia must deepen Asean economic integration, including energy resilience, strengthen intra-Asean trade and investment and enhance connectivity physically, digitally and financially.
